input.prompt:
Build a Blazor page for a multi-step job application form. This has many
distinct visual sections and shared state. Here are the service contracts already registered in DI:

```csharp
public record JobApplication(PersonalInfo Personal, WorkHistory Work, List<Reference> References, string CoverLetter);
public record PersonalInfo(string FullName, string Email, string Phone, string Address);
public record WorkHistory(List<WorkEntry> Entries);
public record WorkEntry(string Company, string Title, DateTime StartDate, DateTime? EndDate, string Description);
public record Reference(string Name, string Relationship, string Email, string Phone);

public interface IApplicationService
{
    Task<int> SubmitApplicationAsync(JobApplication application);
    Task<bool> ValidateEmailAsync(string email);
}
```

The page should have:

**Step indicator bar:**
- Shows all steps: "Personal Info" → "Work History" → "References" → "Cover Letter" → "Review & Submit"
- The current step is highlighted, completed steps show a checkmark, future steps are dimmed
- Users can click a completed step to go back and edit it

**Step 1 — Personal Info:**
- Inputs for full name, email, phone, and address
- Email is validated via ValidateEmailAsync when the user moves to the next step
- A "Next" button that validates required fields before advancing

**Step 2 — Work History:**
- A dynamic list of work entries — start with one empty entry
- Each entry has: company name, job title, start date, end date (optional, checkbox for "current"), description
- "Add Another Position" button to add more entries
- "Remove" button on each entry (except if it's the only one)
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 3 — References:**
- Space for 2-3 references, each with: name, relationship, email, phone
- "Add Reference" button (max 3)
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 4 — Cover Letter:**
- A large textarea for the cover letter
- A character count display (e.g., "342 / 2000 characters")
- "Back" and "Next" buttons

**Step 5 — Review & Submit:**
- Read-only summary of all entered data organized by section
- An "Edit" link next to each section that jumps back to that step
- A "Submit Application" button that calls SubmitApplicationAsync
- After submission, show a confirmation with the application ID

Route: `/apply`

  and data flow.

  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown
## Component Plan: [Feature Name]

### Component Tree
[ASCII tree showing parent-child relationships]

### Component Table
| Component | Action | Render Mode | Purpose | Est. Lines |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Data Flow
[State owner] → [Parameters down] → [EventCallbacks up]

### Implementation Order
1. [First file to create — why]
2. [Second file — why]
...
```

After outputting the plan, **immediately begin implementing** the components in the order listed. Do not wait for approval or ask "shall I proceed?" — the plan is a guide for you to follow, not a proposal for the user to approve.

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

| Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | Correct Approach |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| One page component with 500+ lines | Impossible to test, reuse, or maintain | Decompose into focused components |
| Passing 10+ parameters through intermediate components | Parameter drilling obscures intent | Use cascading values or a scoped state service |
| Child component fetching its own data from an API | Multiple components making redundant calls | Parent owns data, passes via parameters |
| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |
| Generic components for one-off usage | Over-engineering adds complexity | Only extract generics when reuse is proven |

## Guidelines

- **Plan briefly, then implement.** Write a concise component table and data flow map, then immediately create the `.razor` files — never stop at just the plan.
- **Prefer many small components over one large one.** A component with a single clear purpose is easier to understand, test, and reuse.
- **State ownership is the first decision.** Before writing fetch logic, decide which component owns the data.
- **Build bottom-up.** Create leaf components first so parent components can reference them immediately.
- **Name components after what they render**, not what they do internally: `ProductCard` not `ProductRenderer`, `OrderFilters` not `FilterHandler`.

  and data flow.
  DO NOT USE FOR: creating new Blazor projects or apps from scratch
  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown

### Component Tree

| Component | Action | Render Mode | Purpose | Est. Lines |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Data Flow

2. [Second file — why]
...
```

| Child component fetching its own data from an API | Multiple components making redundant calls | Parent owns data, passes via parameters |
| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |

Let me plan the component decomposition for this multi-step job application form, then implement it.

## Component Plan: Job Application Form

### Component Tree
```
Apply                        (page — owns all form state, orchestrates steps)
├── StepIndicator            (shows progress bar with step names, checkmarks)
├── PersonalInfoStep         (step 1 — personal info fields)
├── WorkHistoryStep          (step 2 — dynamic list of work entries)
│   └── WorkEntryEditor      (single work entry form)
├── ReferencesStep           (step 3 — 2-3 references)


│   └── ReferenceEditor      (single reference form)
├── CoverLetterStep          (step 4 — textarea with char count)
└── ReviewStep               (step 5 — read-only summary + submit)
```

### Data Flow
The Apply page manages all the form state and orchestrates navigation between steps. The StepIndicator displays progress and handles step navigation, while each step component manages its own data and communicates changes back up. The ReviewStep collects everything together for a final read-through before submission.

data.content:
Now I have a clear picture of the project. Let me plan the component decomposition and implement it.

## Component Plan: Job Application Form

### Component Tree
```
Apply.razor                     (page — owns all state, orchestrates steps)
├── StepIndicator.razor         (progress bar, clickable completed steps)
├── PersonalInfoStep.razor      (step 1 — name, email, phone, address)
├── WorkHistoryStep.razor       (step 2 — dynamic work entry list)
│   └── WorkEntryEditor.razor   (single work entry card)
├── ReferencesStep.razor        (step 3 — 2-3 references)
│   └── ReferenceEditor.razor   (single reference card)
├── CoverLetterStep.razor       (step 4 — textarea + char count)
└── ReviewStep.razor            (step 5 — summary + submit)
```

### Data Flow
- `Apply` owns: currentStep, completedSteps, PersonalInfoModel, WorkEntries, References, coverLetter, submission state
- Mutable class models passed by reference → child mutations visible to parent
- `coverLetter` (string) uses `EventCallback<string>` for changes
- `EventCallback` flows up: OnNext, OnBack, OnEdit, OnSubmit

### Implementation Order
1. Models → 2. Leaf editors → 3. Step components → 4. Page

Let me implement everything now.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a good understanding of the project. Let me plan the component decomposition and implement it.

## Component Plan

### Component Tree
```
Apply.razor                     (page — owns all form state, orchestrates steps)
├── StepIndicator.razor         (progress bar with step names, checkmarks, click-to-navigate)
├── PersonalInfoStep.razor      (step 1 — name, email, phone, address)
├── WorkHistoryStep.razor       (step 2 — dynamic list of work entries)
│   └── WorkEntryEditor.razor   (single work entry form)
├── ReferencesStep.razor        (step 3 — 2-3 references)


│   └── ReferenceEditor.razor   (single reference form)
├── CoverLetterStep.razor       (step 4 — textarea with char count)
└── ReviewStep.razor            (step 5 — read-only summary + submit)
```

### Data Flow
The Apply page holds all the state: current step, completed steps, and the form data models for personal info, work history, references, and cover letter. Each step component receives its data as a parameter and communicates back through event callbacks, with StepIndicator handling navigation between steps and the other components managing their specific form sections.

### Implementation Order
I need to start with the models and service interface, then build out the leaf components like WorkEntryEditor and ReferenceEditor, followed by StepIndicator, and work my way up to the container components.

For the form binding, I'll need mutable model classes since the records are immutable—I'll convert them to records when submitting. Let me organize this by creating the models file first with both the records and the mutable form models, then set up the component directory structure. I'll organize the models and components into a clear folder structure. I need to create the application models as records, mutable form models for binding, and then build out all the Razor components for each step of the job application form, including the main Apply page and the step indicator.

For the mutable models, I'm defining plain classes with auto-properties that the Apply page can bind to, then assemble them into immutable records when submitting.

Defining the mutable model classes... Writing component markup... I need to set up the mutable form models alongside the records the user provided, then write out all the component files. For the step indicator, I'm creating a visual progress tracker with numbered circles, connecting lines, step labels, and styling that shows completed steps with checkmarks, highlights the current step, and dims future ones using Bootstrap 5 and scoped CSS. Then I'll handle the state management in the Apply page to track the current step, completed steps, and the form data across all five sections.

Writing the models and components... Defining form model classes that mirror the domain records, with conversion methods to transform the mutable models back into immutable records for submission.

Now I'm building out the Razor components, starting with a step indicator to guide users through the form sections. Still writing component styles... Still writing styles... Building out the form fields... Completing the validation logic...

Now I'm building the WorkEntryEditor component with a card layout that displays position information and allows users to remove entries if permitted, along with form fields for company details. Building the form fields... Building the work history component... Writing component validation... Building the form layout... Completing the component template with the reference loop, showing an add button when under the limit, and navigation buttons at the bottom, then defining the code-behind with parameters for the references list and callbacks for navigation and removal actions. Writing component markup... I need to handle two-way binding properly since strings are immutable in C# — when the textarea updates the CoverLetter parameter, the parent won't see the change unless I invoke the CoverLetterChanged callback. I'll use a local backing field that syncs with the parameter to manage this correctly.

Setting up the component with an `@oninput` handler... I'm reconsidering the binding approach for the textarea — `@bind:get` and `@bind:set` are for component parameters from the parent, not for local textarea binding. Instead, I'll use a standard input binding with a value attribute and an `oninput` event handler to sync changes back to the parent through an EventCallback.

Now I'm moving on to the ReviewStep component, which needs to display all the collected data in read-only format with edit links for each section. I'm building out the personal information section with a header and edit button, then displaying the user's details like name, email, phone, and address in a definition list format. Continuing with the references section, displaying each reference entry with contact information and adding an edit button for that section. Adding the submission controls with a back button and a submit button that shows a loading spinner while the form is being processed, then wrapping up the component with its parameter definitions. Building the Apply page component... Finishing up the review step with submission handling, then closing out the switch statement. Now I'm setting up the component's code section with state variables for tracking the current step, completed steps, and form data like personal info, work entries, references, and cover letter, along with submission status flags. I'm also starting to define the navigation logic to move between steps.

Writing the submission handler... I need to make sure the _Imports.razor file includes the Models namespace so the JobApplication components can access them properly, then I'll generate all the necessary files.

data.content:
Build succeeded with 0 errors and 0 warnings. Here's what was created:

**12 files** across a clean component hierarchy:

```
Models/
  ApplicationModels.cs          — Records + IApplicationService interface
  FormModels.cs                 — Mutable form-binding classes

Components/Pages/JobApplication/
  Apply.razor                   — Page (@page "/apply"), owns all state
  StepIndicator.razor + .css    — Progress bar with clickable completed steps
  PersonalInfoStep.razor        — Step 1: name/email/phone/address + async email validation
  WorkEntryEditor.razor         — Single work entry card (leaf)
  WorkHistoryStep.razor         — Step 2: dynamic list of work entries
  ReferenceEditor.razor         — Single reference card (leaf)
  ReferencesStep.razor          — Step 3: 2-3 references
  CoverLetterStep.razor         — Step 4: textarea + character counter
  ReviewStep.razor              — Step 5: read-only summary + submit + confirmation
```

**Key design decisions:**
- Mutable class models (`PersonalInfoModel`, etc.) passed by reference — child mutations are visible to the parent without callbacks
- `coverLetter` (string/immutable) uses `EventCallback<string>` for parent sync
- Each step validates required fields before allowing "Next"
- Step indicator supports click-to-navigate on completed steps only
- `IApplicationService` is injected via DI — just register your implementation in `Program.cs`
